one_line_takeaway: Two LoRA adapters that each look and behave safely on their own can, once linearly merged as recommended by an attacker, flip a safety-aligned LLM into complying with harmful requests under ordinary prompts, and this composition-triggered failure evades tested static defenses (SafeLoRA, PEFTGuard) because no single adapter carries a detectable malicious signature.
key_finding: Individual colluding adapters remain near-baseline safe (0.0% ASR on AdvBench for both A1 and A2 alone, across all three models under both LlamaGuard3 and HarmBench-CLS), while the composed state produces a sharp jump in harmful compliance: on Qwen2.5-7B, AdvBench ASR rises from 0.0% (individual) to 100.0% (LlamaGuard3) / 91.1% (HarmBench-CLS) when A1+A2 are merged, and on HarmBench itself rises to 95.3%/68.6%; Llama3-8B reaches 98.2% ASR on AdvBench (LlamaGuard3) in the composed state; Gemma2-2B reaches 98.2%/80.4% on AdvBench. FRR on benign Alpaca prompts stays low (0.4%-3.0%) in all configurations, and MMLU-5-shot / Wikitext-2 perplexity change negligibly across individual and composed states (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B MMLU 73.42 base vs 74.23 composed; Llama3-8B 67.76 base vs 67.40 composed), showing the attack preserves utility and coherence. Task-specific perplexity confirms each adapter genuinely improves on its intended anchor domain (Llama3-8B: Shakespeare-Dolly perplexity 15.91->5.64 with A1; GSM8K perplexity 3.49->2.03 with A2), supporting the 'plausibility camouflage' claim. The compositional-specificity control shows mismatched merges (benign adapter B + A1, or B + A2) do NOT trigger refusal suppression (ASR 16.4% and 4.3% respectively vs. base ASR 5.6%), confirming the effect requires the specific colluding pair, not just any merge. SafeLoRA weight-space projection scores for the colluding adapters fall within the benign-harmful range without clean separation, and PEFTGuard (Appendix C), trained on PADBench, classifies both individual colluding adapters as benign -- i.e., two representative static/weight-space defenses fail to flag the adapters pre-merge. The loss-landscape sweep (varying merge coefficients s1,s2) shows the composed state sits in a low harmful-compliance-loss basin and a high refusal-loss region that individual adapters do not occupy. The N-adapter scaling study (Table 5, Llama3-8B) shows that as the number of colluding adapters increases from N=2 to N=4, individual-adapter average ASR drops further (1.3% at N=2, 0.6% at N=3, 0.5% at N=4) while colluding ASR stays high (60.5%, 65.4%, 65.1%), with diminishing attack-efficacy gains beyond N=3, leading the authors to suggest N=2 or N=3 as the best attacker efficacy/deployment-complexity tradeoff.
core_contribution: The paper (Sihao Ding, Mercedes-Benz Research & Development North America) identifies and empirically demonstrates a compositional vulnerability in modular LLM safety alignment, called Colluding LoRA (CoLoRA): a pair of LoRA adapters that each pass individually as benign, safe, and functionally plausible, but which jointly suppress the base model's refusal behavior once linearly merged, causing compliance with harmful requests under standard, unmodified prompts. This is the paper's own construction and experimental contribution, not a survey of prior backdoor work.
threat_model: A supply-chain adversary uploads adapters to a public LoRA repository. The adversary has white-box access to a safety-aligned base model W0 and can optimize adapter weights offline, but cannot modify the repository's scanning pipeline, the base model weights, or the user's inference code. The adversary crafts two (or more) adapters designed to look benign and useful in isolation (e.g., presented as complementary utilities, such as a style-transfer adapter and a math adapter) so that a downstream user is likely to combine them via standard linear LoRA merging. Harmful behavior activates only in the merged/composed weight state (W0+ΔW1+ΔW2), not via any input-side trigger, rare token, or adversarial suffix -- distinguishing it from prompt jailbreaks, single-artifact fine-tuning attacks, and prior composite/input-triggered backdoors (Table 6 in the paper explicitly contrasts CoLoRA against Prompt Jailbreak, Fine-Tuning Attack, and Composite Backdoor along axis of injection point, trigger mechanism, benign-in-isolation, and prompt-specificity).
limitations: The paper's threat model requires white-box access to the base model to optimize adapters offline, and requires a downstream user to actually merge exactly the attacker's designated adapter set together (not just download them individually) -- the paper itself notes as N grows, 'the likelihood of a user assembling the full set of necessary components decreases.' Evaluation is limited to three open-weight instruct models (Gemma2-2B, Qwen2.5-7B, Llama3-8B) and standard linear LoRA merging; the paper does not test other merging strategies (e.g., weighted/TIES-style merging) beyond a coefficient sweep for the loss-landscape visualization, nor larger/production-scale models. Only two static/weight-space defenses (SafeLoRA, PEFTGuard) were tested, both were evaded, but the paper does not evaluate more sophisticated composition-aware or behavioral (runtime) detection methods, since -- per the authors -- none yet exist for this specific threat. The paper is authored from an industry (Mercedes-Benz R&D) affiliation, is a single-author work, and the ethics statement notes the authors did not deploy harmful adapters in real public repositories, so real-world repository-scale detection/evasion is not empirically tested end-to-end.

quantitative_results: Qwen2.5-7B AdvBench ASR: 0.0% (A1 alone), 0.0% (A2 alone), 100.0% (A1+A2, LlamaGuard3) / 91.1% (A1+A2, HarmBench-CLS)
FRR on Alpaca across all configs/models: 0.4%-3.0%
Qwen2.5-7B MMLU 5-shot: 73.42 (base) vs 74.23 (A1+A2 composed); Wikitext-2 PPL 6.79 (base) vs 6.63 (composed)
Llama3-8B MMLU 5-shot: 67.76 (base) vs 67.40 (composed); Wikitext-2 PPL 7.57 (base) vs 7.10 (composed)
Llama3-8B task-specific perplexity: Shakespeare-Dolly 15.91 (base) -> 5.64 (with A1); GSM8K 3.49 (base) -> 2.03 (with A2)

Compositional specificity control (Llama3-8B): base ASR 5.6%, benign adapter B alone ASR 16.0%, B+A1 ASR 16.4%, B+A2 ASR 4.3% (mismatched merges do not trigger suppression, vs. valid pair's ~90-98% ASR)

Scaling N colluding adapters (Llama3-8B, Table 5): N=2 individual avg ASR 1.3% / colluding ASR 60.5%; N=3 individual avg ASR 0.6% / colluding ASR 65.4%; N=4 individual avg ASR 0.5% / colluding ASR 65.1%
LoRA trainable parameters: Gemma2-2B-It 12.8M (0.49% of total), Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct 20.2M (0.27%), Llama3-8B-Instruct 27.3M (0.34%)
PEFTGuard (Appendix C) classified both individual colluding adapters (A1, A2) as benign for Llama3-8B
baselines_compared: PEFTGuard (static backdoor classifier trained on PADBench)
unrelated benign 'baseline adapter B' (CodeAlpaca-trained) used as a compositional-specificity control
